question: A telehealth startup runs a HIPAA-regulated patient-portal API on EC2 behind an Application Load Balancer in a single VPC. Auditors require that any administrative SSH or RDP session into the instances be recorded keystroke-by-keystroke and that no inbound TCP/22 or TCP/3389 ports are ever opened to the internet or to corporate VPN ranges. Which approach satisfies both requirements with the least operational burden?
options: A. Place the instances in a private subnet, install an SSH bastion in a public subnet protected by a security-group rule limited to the office CIDR, and enable VPC Flow Logs to S3.

B. Use AWS Systems Manager Session Manager with session logging streamed to a CloudWatch Logs group encrypted by a customer-managed KMS key, and remove all 22/3389 ingress rules from the instance security groups.
C. Deploy AWS Client VPN, require certificate-based mutual auth, then SSH from the VPN-assigned IP range; enable Client VPN connection logs.
D. Configure EC2 Instance Connect with IAM-based ephemeral keys and rely on CloudTrail data events to record the keystrokes inside each session.
explanation: Session Manager tunnels through the SSM agent over outbound HTTPS, so no inbound 22/3389 is needed, and it natively streams full session transcripts to CloudWatch Logs or S3 with optional KMS encryption. A bastion still requires open 22 ingress somewhere. Client VPN avoids the internet but does not record keystrokes. EC2 Instance Connect logs the API call but not in-session keystrokes — CloudTrail data events do not capture shell IO.

question: A fintech firm operates 14 AWS accounts inside an AWS Organization. The CISO wants every new account, present and future, to have CloudTrail enabled with log-file validation, GuardDuty turned on in all enabled Regions, and an S3 block-public-access baseline — all centrally managed and impossible for the account owner to disable. Which combination achieves this with the minimum custom code?
options: A. A Lambda in the management account that runs nightly, assumes OrganizationAccountAccessRole into each member, and re-applies the desired settings.
B. A StackSet using SELF_MANAGED permissions deployed to every OU, plus a Service Control Policy that denies cloudtrail:Stop* and guardduty:Delete*.
C. AWS Control Tower landing zone with the mandatory and strongly-recommended guardrails enabled, supplemented by an SCP denying disable actions on the regulated services.
D. AWS Config conformance pack deployed organization-wide, with auto-remediation SSM documents that re-enable any disabled service.
explanation: Control Tower automates enrolment of new accounts, deploys the CloudTrail/Config baseline, and its preventative SCP guardrails block disabling those services without writing custom Lambda. A StackSet plus SCP is workable but Control Tower already wraps that pattern and handles new-account drift. Config conformance packs detect/remediate but don't prevent disablement at API-call time. The Lambda sweeper is reactive and brittle.

question: A gaming company encrypts player save data in S3 using SSE-KMS with a customer-managed key. A new requirement says the security team must be able to revoke access to all save data within 60 seconds in case of a breach, without deleting any objects and without coordinating with every IAM principal. What design meets this requirement?
options: A. Set the bucket's default encryption to SSE-S3 instead, and rotate the bucket policy when revocation is needed.
B. Continue using the existing CMK; on a breach, disable the CMK in KMS — every subsequent decrypt call will fail across all principals.
C. Use S3 Object Lock in compliance mode so objects cannot be read during the lock period.
D. Apply a bucket policy with an aws:CurrentTime condition that the security team can shorten on demand.
explanation: Disabling the CMK is a single API call that immediately makes every encrypted object unreadable, regardless of which principal holds s3:GetObject. SSE-S3 removes the kill-switch. Object Lock prevents deletion/overwrite, not reads. Time-condition bucket policies don't override IAM identity-based grants and are easily bypassed by principals with broader policies.

question: An e-commerce site terminates TLS at an Application Load Balancer in front of EC2. Compliance requires that the connection between the ALB and the EC2 instances also be encrypted, that the certificate on each instance be automatically renewed, and that no private keys ever leave the instances. Which approach is the simplest fit?
options: A. Issue a single wildcard cert from a public CA, distribute it to every instance via SSM Parameter Store, and have a cron job pull updates.
B. Use ACM Private CA to issue certs, install the ACM agent on instances via SSM, and configure the ALB target group to use HTTPS to the targets.
C. Generate self-signed certs at instance boot in user-data and accept them at the ALB by importing the public key into ACM.
D. Terminate TLS on a Network Load Balancer with a public ACM cert, then use Nitro Enclaves on each instance to handle re-encryption.
explanation: ACM Private CA can issue per-instance certs, and the ACM agent (or Systems Manager-managed renewal) handles automatic rotation while the private key stays on the instance. The wildcard-cert approach requires manual rotation and the key is shared. Self-signed certs aren't trusted by ACM at all. Nitro Enclaves are for sensitive in-memory processing, not for ALB-to-EC2 cert management.

question: A SaaS vendor exposes a multi-tenant API. Each tenant has thousands of end-users who authenticate via the tenant's own corporate IdP (variously Okta, Entra ID, or Google Workspace). The vendor wants short-lived AWS credentials issued to a per-tenant IAM role so backend Lambda functions can scope DynamoDB access by tenant. Which identity design is appropriate?
options: A. Cognito User Pools with one pool per tenant and federated SAML providers per tenant; map pool groups to IAM roles via a Cognito Identity Pool.
B. A single IAM user per tenant whose long-term access keys are vended to the customer's IdP via a custom OAuth bridge.
C. SSO Admin (IAM Identity Center) with one permission set per tenant and shared end-user accounts.
D. AWS Directory Service Simple AD with trust relationships into each tenant's IdP, then ec2:AssumeRole from on-prem.
explanation: Cognito User Pools support multiple SAML/OIDC IdPs per pool and the Identity Pool can map a group claim to an IAM role, returning short-lived STS credentials per request. Long-term IAM-user keys are exactly what AWS recommends against. IAM Identity Center is for workforce SSO into AWS consoles, not for end-user customer identities at scale. Simple AD doesn't federate to external IdPs.

question: An IoT platform ingests telemetry from 200,000 field sensors. Each sensor must authenticate to AWS IoT Core with a unique X.509 certificate, and revoking a single compromised sensor must take effect within minutes without redeploying firmware to the rest of the fleet. Which approach meets this?
options: A. Provision a single shared certificate baked into firmware; on compromise, rotate firmware OTA.
B. Use AWS IoT Core registered CA and per-device certificates, then deactivate the offending certificate via UpdateCertificate; IoT Core blocks new connections immediately.
C. Use SigV4 signed requests with one IAM user per device.
D. Use Cognito Identity Pools to issue device credentials, and disable the compromised user.
explanation: IoT Core supports per-device X.509 certs registered against a CA; deactivating a cert takes effect on the next connection attempt and existing sessions are dropped, so revocation is fast and granular. A shared cert means OTA-rotating the entire fleet on a single compromise. IAM users at 200k scale exceeds account quotas and is operationally insane. Cognito Identity Pools are designed for human end-users, not embedded device fleets.

question: A logistics company stores driver license images in S3. A regulator requires that any object older than 30 days be stored on media where neither the AWS account root user nor any IAM principal can delete or overwrite it for 7 years. Which configuration enforces this?
options: A. S3 Object Lock in governance mode with a 7-year retention period and a lifecycle rule moving objects to Glacier Deep Archive after 30 days.
B. S3 Object Lock in compliance mode with a 7-year retention period, applied via a default bucket retention configuration; combine with versioning.
C. SSE-KMS with a key policy that denies kms:Decrypt to all principals after 30 days.
D. Replicate the bucket to a second account and revoke the source account's access via SCP.
explanation: Compliance-mode Object Lock cannot be shortened or removed even by the root user; governance mode permits override by privileged principals (s3:BypassGovernanceRetention), so it doesn't meet the requirement. KMS key-policy timing tricks don't prevent overwrite/delete operations. Cross-account replication is useful for resilience but doesn't lock the source.

question: A healthcare API hosted on EC2 must call Amazon Comprehend Medical with no traffic ever traversing the public internet. The VPC has no NAT gateway and no internet gateway. Which configuration enables this with the least cost?
options: A. Add a NAT gateway in a public subnet so the EC2 instances can reach Comprehend Medical's public endpoint.
B. Create an interface VPC endpoint (powered by AWS PrivateLink) for Comprehend Medical and update the application to use the endpoint DNS.
C. Peer the VPC with an AWS-managed VPC that hosts Comprehend Medical.
D. Use an S3 gateway endpoint as a transit path to Comprehend Medical.
explanation: Interface endpoints expose AWS services privately inside the VPC over PrivateLink — Comprehend Medical supports this. NAT gateway sends traffic over the public internet (and adds an internet gateway requirement). You cannot peer with an AWS service VPC. S3 gateway endpoints route only to S3/DynamoDB and aren't transit paths.

question: A government tenant runs a workload across two AWS accounts joined to an Organization. A new SCP attached to the OU denies all S3 actions in regions other than us-gov-west-1. Developers report that their CI role in account A can still PutObject to a bucket in us-east-1. What is the most likely cause?
options: A. The CI role has an inline policy that explicitly allows s3:PutObject — explicit allows override SCPs.
B. SCPs do not apply to the management account, and the CI role lives there.
C. The bucket policy in us-east-1 grants public access, which bypasses the SCP.
D. SCPs are only enforced on resource policies, not on identity policies.
explanation: SCPs filter the effective permissions of every account in the OU EXCEPT the Organization's management (payer) account, which is unaffected. Inline allow policies do not override SCP denies — SCPs are a permissions ceiling, not a floor. Public buckets don't bypass the SCP attached to the calling principal. SCPs apply to identity policies as well as resource policies.

question: A bank uses Aurora PostgreSQL to store customer PII. Auditors require that database credentials never appear in application config, that they rotate every 30 days, and that the rotation be performed without downtime. Which approach is appropriate?
options: A. Store the credentials in an encrypted SSM Parameter Store SecureString and rotate them with a Lambda triggered by an EventBridge schedule.
B. Use AWS Secrets Manager with the built-in single-user rotation Lambda template for Aurora; configure a 30-day rotation interval.
C. Use IAM database authentication exclusively and rely on STS token expiry for rotation.
D. Bake the credentials into a KMS-encrypted EBS snapshot mounted at instance boot.
explanation: Secrets Manager natively rotates Aurora credentials via AWS-provided Lambda templates and updates the secret atomically; clients fetch the latest version, so there's no downtime. SSM Parameter Store has no native rotation for RDS. IAM DB auth uses 15-minute tokens — not really 'rotation' of stored credentials, and many drivers still pin a connection. EBS snapshots aren't a credential-storage mechanism.

question: A media-streaming service uses CloudFront in front of an S3 origin. A growing fraction of traffic is being scraped by bots that rotate IP addresses across hundreds of /24 ranges and also forge a 'Googlebot' user-agent string. Which control most directly mitigates this?
options: A. AWS Shield Advanced subscription on the CloudFront distribution.
B. A WAF web ACL with the AWS-managed Bot Control rule group, including the Targeted feature set, attached to the CloudFront distribution.
C. An S3 bucket policy that denies requests whose User-Agent starts with 'Googlebot'.
D. A Lambda@Edge function that does a reverse-DNS lookup on every request.
explanation: WAF Bot Control fingerprints clients beyond IP/UA — it can validate that a 'Googlebot' really resolves back to a Google range, and the Targeted set adds challenge interstitials and TLS fingerprinting against IP-rotating bots. Shield Advanced is for DDoS, not scrapers. The S3 bucket policy fires after CloudFront and only matches the literal UA string. A Lambda@Edge reverse-DNS check is reinventing what Bot Control already does, and adds latency on every request.

question: A fintech company wants to give its data-science team query access to a curated subset of S3-hosted parquet files containing customer data, but column-level masking must hide the SSN and DOB columns from anyone whose IAM principal isn't tagged with the 'pii=cleared' attribute. Which service combination implements this most directly?
options: A. Athena with a workgroup that has a query-results bucket policy denying SELECT of the sensitive columns.
B. AWS Lake Formation with column-level permissions and LF-Tags evaluated against the principal's session tags, queried through Athena.
C. S3 Access Points with a Lambda transform that strips columns at GetObject time.
D. Glue crawlers configured to skip the sensitive columns when building the catalog.
explanation: Lake Formation is the only AWS-native control plane that supports column-level (and row-level) authorization on S3-backed tables, with LF-Tag conditions that can incorporate session tags. Athena workgroup policies operate at query/result level, not column. S3 Object Lambda is intended for object transformation but doesn't natively understand parquet schemas or principal attributes. Skipping columns in the crawler hides them from everyone.

question: An online-retail site receives complaints that customer-account email addresses are being scraped via the registration-error response. The endpoint is /signup on a public ALB. Which mitigation directly addresses this without rewriting the application?
options: A. Enable AWS WAF rate-based rules limiting requests per source IP and add a managed Account Takeover Prevention rule group.
B. Move the ALB into a private subnet behind CloudFront.
C. Disable HTTP/2 on the ALB.
D. Use Cognito hosted UI for sign-up with built-in CAPTCHA, fronted by CloudFront with origin access control.
explanation: The Cognito hosted UI provides built-in CAPTCHA challenges and account-takeover protections against username/email enumeration without any code changes — and it offloads the registration response logic from your app. WAF rate-based rules slow scraping but don't fix the enumeration leak in the response body. Hiding the ALB behind CloudFront doesn't change response semantics. HTTP/2 has no relevance.

question: A pharmaceutical research team uses an EC2-based Jupyter environment. Researchers must upload datasets up to 200 GB into S3 from on-premises laptops over the public internet, with end-to-end encryption and integrity verification, but the team lead does not want to issue any long-term IAM access keys. What is the simplest design?
options: A. Issue each researcher a federated console role and have them use the S3 console upload for objects up to 200 GB.
B. Use AWS Transfer Family SFTP with public-key auth tied to an IAM Identity Center identity, backed by S3.
C. Distribute the AWS CLI v2 configured with IAM Identity Center sso login, using aws s3 cp with --sse aws:kms; the CLI handles multipart and integrity checksums.
D. Generate temporary STS keys per user with an Excel macro and email them weekly.
explanation: AWS CLI v2 supports IAM Identity Center login that mints short-lived STS credentials per session, and aws s3 cp transparently does multipart uploads with integrity checksums and supports --sse aws:kms for KMS-encrypted PUTs. The console upload caps single-PUT at 5 GB and doesn't fit multi-hundred-GB workflows well. Transfer Family is fine but expensive overkill for a small team. Emailed STS keys are still long-lived enough to be a credential leak risk and operationally awful.

question: Three teams in the same Organization need to share a centrally-managed VPC: a 'shared services' account hosts a Transit Gateway and a private DNS resolver, while two consumer accounts run workloads. The security team wants the consumers to deploy EC2 into subnets in the shared services account's VPC without granting the consumers IAM rights in that account. Which feature is appropriate?
options: A. VPC peering with route-table propagation in both directions.
B. AWS Resource Access Manager (RAM) sharing the subnets with each consumer account.
C. AWS PrivateLink endpoint services exposing each subnet as a service.
D. Cross-account IAM roles allowing ec2:RunInstances in the shared services account.
explanation: RAM lets the owning account share specific subnets with other accounts in the Organization; consumers see those subnets in their own console and can run instances inside them while networking and route tables remain centrally managed. VPC peering creates separate VPCs. PrivateLink is service-to-service connectivity, not subnet sharing. Cross-account roles work but require granting broad EC2 rights in the central account, contradicting the requirement.

question: An online learning platform hosts video assets in S3 and serves them via CloudFront. The legal team mandates that signed video URLs expire after 4 hours and that revoking a single user's access takes effect immediately, even on URLs that were already issued. Which mechanism meets both?
options: A. CloudFront signed URLs with a 4-hour expiry and rely on the expiry alone.
B. CloudFront signed cookies issued through a Lambda authorizer that checks a DynamoDB revocation list on every viewer request via Lambda@Edge.
C. S3 presigned URLs valid for 4 hours, distributed via CloudFront with origin access control.
D. CloudFront key groups with rotated keys every 4 hours and a key-rotation Lambda.
explanation: Already-issued signed URLs/cookies are valid until their expiry — there is no built-in revocation. A Lambda@Edge viewer-request check against a revocation list is the standard pattern for instant revocation. Plain expiry doesn't allow early revocation. Presigned S3 URLs can't go through CloudFront with OAC reliably. Rotating key groups breaks every outstanding URL, not just one user's.

question: A defense contractor must record every API call across all accounts for at least 7 years, with cryptographic proof that no log file has been altered, and the storage location must be unwritable by anyone except the CloudTrail service itself. Which configuration matches?
options: A. Organization trail with log-file validation enabled, delivering to an S3 bucket in a dedicated log-archive account whose bucket policy denies all PutObject except from the CloudTrail service principal, plus Object Lock in compliance mode for 7 years.
B. Per-account trails delivered to each account's own S3 bucket with default encryption.
C. CloudTrail Lake with default 90-day retention.
D. Forward CloudTrail events to OpenSearch via Kinesis Firehose and snapshot the index nightly.
explanation: An organization trail with log-file validation produces signed digest files that prove integrity, and Object Lock in compliance mode prevents anyone — including root — from altering objects for the retention period. Per-account trails are not centrally controllable and don't enforce immutability. CloudTrail Lake's default retention is short and would need explicit longer retention plus separate immutability — not the most direct fit. OpenSearch indices are mutable.

question: A SaaS product encrypts customer data with KMS. A particular enterprise customer demands that they hold the root of trust — AWS must not be able to decrypt their data even with internal AWS administrative privileges. Which option satisfies this while still allowing the SaaS application to encrypt/decrypt during normal operation?
options: A. AWS-managed KMS key in the customer's account, shared via a key policy.
B. Customer-managed KMS key with imported key material from the customer's HSM, configured to expire if not re-imported.
C. AWS CloudHSM cluster owned by the customer; the SaaS application uses the cluster for crypto operations.
D. SSE-S3 with the customer rotating bucket policies.
explanation: CloudHSM gives the customer single-tenant FIPS-140-3 Level 3 hardware modules where AWS operators have no access to the keys. KMS with imported material still uses the AWS-managed KMS HSM substrate. AWS-managed keys are by definition under AWS control. SSE-S3 keys are entirely AWS-managed.

question: A retail chain is rolling out 600 store-level VPN tunnels back to a hub VPC. Each store needs sub-second failover if the primary tunnel drops, and the security team requires that traffic be encrypted in transit using IKEv2 with PFS. The network team does not want to manage 600 VPN concentrators. Which design is appropriate?
options: A. AWS Site-to-Site VPN with two tunnels per connection terminating on a Transit Gateway, using BGP-based ECMP and the AWS-managed VPN endpoint.
B. AWS Client VPN endpoints, one per store.
C. Direct Connect with MACsec at each store.
D. Self-managed StrongSwan instances on EC2 in an Auto Scaling group as the VPN concentrator.
explanation: Site-to-Site VPN attached to a Transit Gateway gives two AWS-managed tunnels per connection (active/active), supports BGP fast failover, and IKEv2 with PFS is configurable; the TGW removes the need for store-by-store concentrators. Client VPN is for individual user devices, not site-to-site. Direct Connect with MACsec at 600 sites is cost-prohibitive and not always physically feasible. StrongSwan on EC2 reintroduces concentrator management exactly opposite to the requirement.

question: A SaaS analytics product needs to call APIs in customer-owned AWS accounts to read CloudWatch metrics. Each customer onboarded must grant access in a way that (a) does not require sharing secret credentials, (b) lets them revoke access at any time, and (c) verifies that the request really comes from the SaaS provider. Which design is appropriate?
options: A. The customer creates an IAM user in their account and emails the access keys to the SaaS provider.
B. The customer creates a cross-account IAM role trusting the SaaS provider's account, with an sts:ExternalId condition tied to the customer's tenant ID.
C. The SaaS provider creates an IAM role in its own account that the customer references via account number.
D. The customer enables AWS Resource Access Manager and shares the CloudWatch namespace with the SaaS account.
explanation: The cross-account role with ExternalId is the canonical pattern: the customer can revoke by deleting the role, no shared keys, and the ExternalId blocks 'confused deputy' replay attacks where a different SaaS-provider customer tricks the provider into calling the wrong account. Sharing IAM keys violates AWS guidance. A role in the SaaS account doesn't grant access to the customer's data. RAM doesn't share CloudWatch metrics.

question: A startup's CFO wants every IAM user (including the root user) to be required to use an MFA device, and wants this enforced even if a future admin tries to remove the requirement. Which combination provides the strongest enforcement?
options: A. An IAM password policy requiring MFA, attached to each IAM user's policy.

B. An SCP at the Organization root denying all actions except iam:ChangePassword and a small bootstrap set when aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent is false, plus a documented procedure to attach a hardware MFA to root.
C. A CloudWatch alarm that emails the CFO when MFA is disabled.
D. Enabling MFA on the root user only.
explanation: SCPs restrict member-account principals regardless of their identity policies, and conditioning on aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent=false denies sensitive actions for non-MFA sessions account-wide; root-MFA must be attached out-of-band per AWS guidance. IAM password policies don't enforce MFA. An alarm is reactive. Root-only MFA leaves IAM users unprotected.

question: A satellite-imagery provider serves multi-gigabyte raster tiles via API Gateway -> Lambda -> S3. Customers complain that they receive HTTP 503s under high concurrency during eclipses (peak demand events). The Lambda integration is using REST API with proxy integration. Security requires that all requests be authenticated with a per-customer API key. What is the most cost-effective architectural change?
options: A. Move from REST API to HTTP API with a Lambda proxy integration; HTTP API has higher base throughput and lower per-request cost while still supporting API keys via a Lambda authorizer.
B. Replace API Gateway with a public ALB; add WAF for auth.
C. Use a CloudFront distribution with an S3 origin and signed URLs returned by a small Lambda that performs the API-key check.
D. Increase the Lambda's reserved concurrency to 10,000.
explanation: Multi-GB raster tiles should not be streamed through Lambda — Lambda has 6 MB synchronous response limits, and even outside that limit the proxy invocation is wasteful. Returning a CloudFront-signed URL after a tiny auth-check Lambda is the standard 'big binary' pattern, and CloudFront naturally absorbs eclipse-event spikes. HTTP API still streams through Lambda. ALB + WAF doesn't easily implement per-customer API keys. Reserved concurrency doesn't fix the fundamental bandwidth pattern.

question: A regulated insurance company requires that its cloud network be continuously evaluated against a set of CIS-Benchmark configuration rules, with non-compliant resources auto-remediated when possible and reported to a SIEM otherwise. Which combination implements this?
options: A. AWS Config conformance pack mapped to CIS rules, with SSM Automation documents as remediation actions and Config compliance events forwarded to the SIEM via EventBridge.
B. CloudTrail with metric filters on a CloudWatch alarm.
C. Trusted Advisor checks viewed in the console daily by an analyst.
D. AWS Security Hub findings exported manually each month.
explanation: Config conformance packs deploy CIS-style rules, support SSM-based auto-remediation per rule, and stream compliance status changes through EventBridge to any SIEM. CloudTrail metric filters don't evaluate configuration state. Trusted Advisor is broad cost/perf/security advice, not deep CIS coverage. Manual Security Hub export skips the auto-remediation requirement.

question: A travel-booking application stores its session tokens in DynamoDB. The security team wants any read of the session-token table to be logged with the caller's identity, the requested key, and the timestamp, so suspicious lookups can be investigated. Which approach captures this without modifying the application?
options: A. Enable CloudTrail data events for the DynamoDB table, choosing to log read events.
B. Enable DynamoDB Streams in NEW_IMAGE mode.
C. Enable VPC Flow Logs on the application's subnet.
D. Turn on DynamoDB Contributor Insights.
explanation: CloudTrail data events for DynamoDB capture per-item GetItem/Query/Scan calls including the caller's IAM identity, the table, and the key — that's exactly the audit trail required, and it's transparent to the application. DynamoDB Streams emit data changes only, not reads. Flow Logs are network-level, not item-level. Contributor Insights surfaces hot-key patterns, not auditable identity-attributed reads.

question: An online-learning platform runs a stateless Node.js API on EC2 behind an ALB in two AZs. Traffic is highly bursty around exam release times and the team has had two outages caused by a single AZ losing all healthy targets. The team wants the cheapest design that survives a full AZ outage with no human intervention. Which is appropriate?
options: A. Provision twice as many instances in one AZ and rely on ALB cross-zone load balancing.
B. Run an Auto Scaling group spanning three AZs with a minimum capacity that keeps the workload survivable when one AZ is down, with target tracking on request count.
C. Run the workload on a single large instance per AZ with Elastic IPs.
D. Move the API to Lambda behind API Gateway in a single Region.
explanation: A 3-AZ ASG with capacity sized so that the surviving 2 AZs can carry the load is the classic stateless-web-tier pattern; it self-heals AZ failures and target-tracking absorbs bursts. A 2-AZ design with double capacity in one AZ doesn't survive THAT AZ failing. Single instances per AZ have no horizontal scaling. Lambda is fine but a much bigger architectural shift than the question is asking — and Lambda still recommends multi-AZ awareness; the choice between B and D rests on 'cheapest design that survives AZ outage'.

question: A ride-sharing company's order-matching workload publishes events to an SNS topic. Three downstream services (analytics, fraud detection, driver-routing) each must receive every event, and the company cannot afford to lose any during a downstream outage of up to 6 hours. Which design fits?
options: A. SNS topic fan-out to one SQS standard queue per consumer; each consumer reads from its own queue.
B. SNS topic fan-out directly to the three Lambda functions with no queue.
C. EventBridge rules with the three consumers as targets.
D. Kinesis Data Stream with three consumer applications in different shards.
explanation: SNS->SQS fanout decouples each consumer; SQS retains messages up to 14 days by default, so a 6-hour downstream outage is absorbed without loss. SNS direct-to-Lambda will retry briefly then DLQ — without an SQS buffer in front, you risk losing or DLQ'ing during long outages. EventBridge has retry/DLQ but the SNS+SQS pattern is the canonical resilience play and is also cheaper for 3 consumers. Kinesis is pull-based with 24h-365d retention but has shard contention between consumers unless using enhanced fan-out, which costs more.

question: A weather-data company runs a write-heavy time-series ingest at 30,000 writes per second into Aurora MySQL. RPO must be ~5 seconds and RTO under 1 minute even during a Region failure. Which deployment satisfies this?
options: A. A single-Region Aurora cluster with cross-Region read replica.
B. Aurora Global Database with one secondary Region; managed planned failover for RPO ~1s and unplanned failover under 1 minute.
C. RDS MySQL Multi-AZ.
D. Self-managed MySQL on EC2 with binlog replication to a second Region.
explanation: Aurora Global Database is purpose-built for sub-second cross-Region replication and supports managed failover under a minute. A standard cross-Region read replica has higher replication lag and slower promotion. RDS MySQL Multi-AZ is single-Region. Self-managed binlog replication takes weeks of operational tuning to even approach Aurora Global's RPO/RTO and is brittle.

question: A fintech batch system runs a chain of ~40 jobs with branching, retries, and human-approval steps. Some steps run on Lambda, others on Fargate, and some need to wait up to 24 hours for an external webhook. Which orchestration service fits with the least custom plumbing?
options: A. SQS plus a hand-written controller Lambda.
B. AWS Step Functions Standard workflow with task tokens for the webhook waits and Map states for fan-out.
C. Apache Airflow self-hosted on EC2.
D. EventBridge rules chained one to the next.
explanation: Step Functions Standard supports up to 1-year executions, native Lambda and Fargate task integrations, branching/retries/human-approval via task tokens, and visual debugging — purpose-built for this. SQS+Lambda forces you to build retries, branches, and timeouts yourself. Self-hosted Airflow is operational overhead. EventBridge rules don't carry workflow state.

question: A health-records SaaS uses an RDS for PostgreSQL Multi-AZ instance. Tests reveal that a recent hardware-failure event in the primary AZ caused a 90-second downtime, which exceeds the 30-second RTO target. Which change most directly improves failover time?
options: A. Increase the instance class to memory-optimized.
B. Switch to Multi-AZ with two readable standbys (Multi-AZ Cluster) — failover typically under 35 seconds and standbys are usable as read replicas.
C. Add a cross-Region read replica.
D. Enable RDS Proxy in front of the existing instance.
explanation: RDS Proxy holds the application's connection pool and re-establishes connections to the new primary far faster than letting clients reconnect themselves through DNS — empirically cutting Multi-AZ failover-perceived downtime to single-digit seconds. Multi-AZ Cluster (option B) DOES typically fail over in ~35s but that's still over the 30s target without proxy. Memory class doesn't change failover. A cross-Region replica doesn't help intra-Region failover. The intended best answer is RDS Proxy which addresses the connection-handling delay that dominates failover time.

question: A SaaS product replicates an S3 bucket cross-Region for DR. The team finds that during a recent test, replication lag spiked to 90 minutes, blowing past the 5-minute RPO. Which configuration tightens replication time predictably?
options: A. Increase the bucket's storage class to S3 Express One Zone.
B. Enable S3 Replication Time Control (RTC) with its 15-minute SLA.
C. Switch to AWS Backup with a cross-Region copy schedule every 5 minutes.
D. Use DataSync hourly between the two buckets.
explanation: S3 RTC is the only feature offering a contractual replication-time SLA (99.99% of objects in 15 minutes). Express One Zone is single-AZ, low-latency storage — not cross-Region replication. AWS Backup can't schedule every 5 minutes for S3 and isn't designed for that pattern. DataSync at hourly granularity has 60-minute RPO at best.

question: A media-rendering pipeline submits ~50,000 short jobs per day to a Spot fleet. Occasional Spot interruptions are tolerable, but the pipeline has been losing partially-completed work because jobs run for up to 25 minutes. Which approach reduces lost work most cheaply?
options: A. Move the entire fleet to On-Demand.
B. Subscribe to EC2 Spot interruption notices via EventBridge and have the workload checkpoint state to S3 every 2 minutes.
C. Increase the bid price.
D. Use Reserved Instances for the rendering tier.
explanation: Spot interruption notices arrive ~2 minutes ahead of termination — checkpointing on a tighter cadence (or on receipt of the notice) lets the workload resume on a replacement instance with minimal lost work, while keeping Spot's cost advantage. All-On-Demand or RIs sacrifice the cost win. There is no 'bid price' in the modern Spot model — and even when there was, it didn't reduce interruptions, only made the user pay more for the same outcome.

question: An online-classifieds site stores user-uploaded images in EFS shared between many EC2 web servers. The team wants to survive a Region failure with an RPO of 1 hour and an RTO of 4 hours, with the simplest tooling. Which design fits?
options: A. EFS replication to a second Region, configured per file system.
B. Snapshot the EBS volumes hourly and copy to a second Region.
C. Sync EFS to S3 every hour with DataSync, then restore on demand.
D. A self-managed rsync cron job between two EFS file systems via VPC peering.
explanation: EFS Replication is a managed cross-Region replication feature that takes care of the plumbing and offers RPO of around 15 minutes and a quick failover, well within the 1h/4h target. EBS snapshots don't apply to EFS data. DataSync to S3 is a viable but more complex pattern needing restore-back logic on failover. rsync over peering is self-managed and brittle.

question: A national grocery chain runs an order-management API on ECS with Fargate. During major sale events, the API briefly returns 5xx errors because downstream payment-processor calls time out, and customers retry, amplifying load. Which architectural pattern most reduces this 'retry storm'?
options: A. Increase Fargate task count and CPU.
B. Place the API behind an SQS queue for synchronous calls.
C. Implement exponential backoff with jitter in the API's payment-processor client and add a circuit breaker that fails fast when the downstream is unhealthy.
D. Switch from Fargate to EC2 launch type.
explanation: Retry storms are best mitigated with backoff+jitter and a circuit breaker that returns 'service unavailable' immediately when the downstream is failing, so callers don't pile on. Adding more Fargate tasks doesn't help if the downstream is the bottleneck. SQS in front of an inherently synchronous checkout flow is the wrong shape. EC2 launch type doesn't change the pattern.

question: A document-processing workload accepts file uploads up to 100 MB into S3 and then runs a 30-minute analysis on each. The team wants every uploaded file processed exactly once, even if the trigger fires twice. Which design achieves this most cleanly?
options: A. S3 event notification to SNS, fan out to multiple Lambdas.
B. S3 event notification to an SQS FIFO queue with content-based deduplication, consumed by a worker that records processed object keys in DynamoDB with a conditional write.
C. S3 event notification directly to a long-running Lambda configured for 30 minutes.
D. EventBridge rule from S3, target a Step Functions Express workflow.
explanation: FIFO queues with content-based dedup combined with an idempotency key (object ETag + version) tracked in DynamoDB give end-to-end exactly-once semantics. Lambda's 15-minute max prevents 30-minute jobs. SNS fan-out is at-least-once with no dedup. Express workflows are limited to 5 minutes — too short for a 30-minute analysis.

question: A SaaS product uses Route 53 to direct traffic to two equally-sized stacks in us-east-1 and eu-west-1. During a recent us-east-1 outage, customers continued hitting the unhealthy stack for 15 minutes before manual DNS changes redirected them. Which Route 53 feature avoids this?
options: A. Latency-based routing without health checks.
B. Failover routing policy with Route 53 health checks tied to the application endpoint.
C. Geolocation routing.
D. Multivalue answer with a longer TTL.
explanation: Failover (or latency/weighted) with associated health checks automatically removes unhealthy records from responses; convergence happens within the TTL plus check interval (often under 60s). Latency routing alone doesn't react to outages. Geolocation has no health awareness. Multivalue answers with longer TTL make it WORSE — clients cache the bad record longer.

question: A logistics company runs a workload on an EC2 Auto Scaling group that occasionally fails to scale out fast enough during sudden demand spikes (under 60 seconds). Instances take ~3 minutes to boot and warm caches. Which feature reduces cold-start exposure?
options: A. Predictive scaling with a target-tracking policy and warm pools holding instances in a stopped state.
B. A higher CPU target on the existing target-tracking policy.
C. Larger instance types.
D. Cluster placement group.
explanation: Warm pools keep pre-initialized instances ready (stopped or running-but-unallocated), and predictive scaling spins capacity up before the spike — together they collapse 'sudden demand to ready' to seconds. Raising the CPU target makes scale-out trigger LATER, not sooner. Bigger instances still take minutes to boot. Cluster placement groups are about latency between instances, not scaling time.

question: A real-time multiplayer game runs on EC2. Players reconnect every few seconds during gameplay; sustained packet loss above 0.1% is unacceptable. The architect must protect against single-instance failure. Which front-door balancer is appropriate?
options: A. Application Load Balancer with sticky sessions.
B. Network Load Balancer with cross-zone load balancing and target group health checks; place game servers in an Auto Scaling group across AZs.
C. Classic Load Balancer.
D. Global Accelerator with no underlying load balancer.
explanation: NLB operates at L4 with millions of pps and ultra-low latency, which is what real-time UDP/TCP game servers need; combined with multi-AZ ASG it tolerates instance failure. ALB is L7 and adds latency that hurts game traffic. Classic Load Balancer is legacy and inferior to NLB on every axis. Global Accelerator complements but doesn't replace a load balancer; without one, instance failure isn't handled.

question: A digital-bank's customer-events service pushes ~5,000 events/sec into a Kinesis Data Stream with 10 shards. Consumers complain of growing iterator age during evening peaks. Which change improves consumer throughput most directly?
options: A. Reduce the number of shards to consolidate traffic.
B. Switch consumers to enhanced fan-out so each consumer gets a dedicated 2 MB/s/shard pipe.
C. Move the producers to MSK without changing consumers.
D. Increase Kinesis stream retention to 7 days.
explanation: Iterator age means consumers are falling behind. Standard Kinesis consumers share 2 MB/s/shard read throughput; enhanced fan-out gives each registered consumer its own 2 MB/s/shard pipe with sub-200ms delivery. Reducing shards lowers TOTAL throughput. Switching to MSK requires re-architecting producers AND consumers. Longer retention doesn't help live consumers catch up.

question: A government agency deploys a static portal to S3 with CloudFront. They require that during a Region-wide S3 failure, the site automatically falls back to a second-Region S3 origin. Which feature gives them automated origin failover?
options: A. CloudFront Origin Groups with a primary and secondary S3 bucket and configured failure-status codes for failover.
B. Route 53 failover at the S3 website endpoint.
C. Manually swapping the CloudFront distribution's origin during an incident.
D. AWS Global Accelerator with two S3 endpoints.
explanation: CloudFront Origin Groups are exactly the automated origin-failover feature: primary fails on configured 4xx/5xx, secondary takes over for that request. Route 53 failover at S3 is messy because S3 website endpoints don't health-check well. Manual swap defeats 'automated'. Global Accelerator doesn't front S3.

question: A genomics startup runs a 200-node ECS cluster on EC2 spread across two AZs. Sometimes the team's CI bot accidentally drains an entire AZ at once for patch testing, and the cluster becomes overloaded in the surviving AZ. Which configuration prevents that overload most simply?
options: A. Disable cross-zone load balancing on the ALB.
B. Use ECS service capacity providers backed by an Auto Scaling group with managed scaling, and enable cluster Auto Scaling so the surviving AZ scales out automatically when capacity drops.
C. Move all tasks to Fargate Spot.
D. Use a placement strategy of binpack on memory.
explanation: Capacity providers + cluster Auto Scaling react to ECS service-level capacity needs and add EC2 hosts in the surviving AZ when one drains, restoring headroom automatically. Disabling cross-zone makes hot-AZ behaviour worse. Fargate Spot may help but is a bigger architecture shift and Spot interruptions hurt resilience. Binpack placement only changes packing density on existing hosts.

question: A media-archival workflow uploads 4 TB of footage daily into S3 from on-premises Avid editing suites. The team needs guaranteed delivery resumability across flaky links, plus integrity verification, and prefers managed services. Which is appropriate?
options: A. AWS DataSync with a self-managed agent on-prem, scheduled hourly tasks to S3 with checksum verification.
B. AWS Snowball Edge shipped weekly.
C. aws s3 sync from a cron job.
D. SFTP via Transfer Family with manual retries.
explanation: DataSync is purpose-built for incremental, resumable, integrity-verified transfers between on-prem and S3 with bandwidth controls and scheduling. Snowball is for one-time bulk migration, not daily 4 TB streaming. aws s3 sync via cron is fine for small jobs but lacks the bandwidth controls, retry semantics, and verification telemetry of DataSync. SFTP doesn't natively chunk and verify multi-TB transfers.

question: A large e-commerce platform uses DynamoDB for shopping-cart data. They want zero-data-loss DR across two Regions with active-active writes from both sides. Which capability fits?
options: A. DynamoDB Global Tables with multi-Region replication and last-writer-wins conflict resolution.
B. DynamoDB Streams + Lambda hand-replicating to a second Region.
C. AWS Backup cross-Region copies every 15 minutes.
D. Read replicas in another Region.
explanation: Global Tables provide multi-Region active-active replication with sub-second propagation and managed last-writer-wins conflict resolution. Hand-rolling via Streams+Lambda is error-prone and not active-active without lots of additional plumbing. AWS Backup at any cadence has minutes of RPO. DynamoDB doesn't have 'read replicas' per se.

question: A research institute's compute cluster runs MPI jobs that require sub-100-microsecond inter-node latency and consistent 100 Gbps throughput. Which combination delivers this?
options: A. Standard ASG across AZs with placement type 'spread'.
B. EC2 instances using EFA (Elastic Fabric Adapter) within a single cluster placement group.
C. Lambda with provisioned concurrency.
D. ECS Fargate tasks with awsvpc networking.
explanation: EFA bypasses the kernel for HPC/MPI workloads and a cluster placement group keeps instances physically close on the same fabric — that's the documented HPC tight-coupling pattern. Spread placement is the opposite — it intentionally separates instances. Lambda and Fargate don't expose EFA or low-microsecond inter-node networking.

question: A payments processor's nightly reconciliation job fails periodically because it depends on an external partner API that occasionally rate-limits. The job currently fails the entire batch on first 429. Which fix improves resilience without rewriting the partner integration?
options: A. Wrap calls in Step Functions with Retry on the rate-limit error type, exponential backoff, and a Catch state for permanent failure.
B. Add a fixed sleep(5) between requests.
C. Move the job to a larger Lambda memory configuration.
D. Run the job on a c6i.32xlarge for more parallelism.
explanation: Step Functions Retry/Catch states give per-error-type backoff with jitter and explicit failure handling — the canonical recipe for rate-limited partner integrations. Fixed sleeps don't react to actual 429s and waste time when not throttled. Bigger Lambda doesn't fix rate-limit logic. More parallelism makes the rate-limit problem worse.

question: A genomics SaaS hosts databases, queues, and EC2 in a single VPC. The team wants the architecture to survive complete loss of any one AZ with no manual intervention and an RPO under 1 second. Which DB choice is appropriate, given they currently use Postgres?
options: A. RDS PostgreSQL Single-AZ with daily snapshots.
B. RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ Cluster (one writer + two readable standbys across three AZs) with synchronous quorum commits.
C. Aurora PostgreSQL Serverless v1.
D. Self-managed PostgreSQL on a single EC2 instance.
explanation: RDS Multi-AZ Cluster offers a writer plus two readable standbys across three AZs with synchronous-quorum commits — RPO is effectively zero on AZ failure and failover is fast. Single-AZ with daily snapshots violates RPO horribly. Aurora Serverless v1 is end-of-life and not the strict-AZ-failure-survival fit (Aurora itself is good, but the question targets a Postgres user). Self-managed Postgres on one instance has zero AZ resilience.

question: A high-traffic news site has a read-heavy product catalog stored in DynamoDB. Reads are 50,000/sec at peak; the workload is dominated by ~200 hot keys. Costs are high and tail latency spikes during traffic events. Which addition reduces both cost and tail latency?
options: A. Add a DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX) cluster in front of the table.
B. Increase the table's RCUs to twice the current value.
C. Switch the table to On-Demand billing.
D. Add a global secondary index.
explanation: DAX is an in-memory write-through cache for DynamoDB that delivers microsecond reads on hot keys, simultaneously cutting RCU consumption (cost) and tail latency. Doubling RCUs raises cost and doesn't help tail latency. On-Demand pricing makes a hot-key, high-volume read workload more expensive, not less. A GSI is for new query patterns, not caching.

question: A SaaS analytics tool runs interactive ad-hoc SQL over 30 TB of parquet files in S3. Queries usually scan small subsets after filtering. Users complain about slow queries; the team also wants to avoid running an always-on cluster. Which configuration is appropriate?
options: A. Athena with partition projection and partition keys on the typical filter columns.
B. Redshift dc2.large always-on cluster with COPY ingest from S3 nightly.
C. EMR cluster running Presto, scaled to zero on weekends.
D. Aurora MySQL with the parquet files imported.
explanation: Athena is serverless (no idle cost), and partition projection plus parquet's columnar pushdown drastically cut bytes scanned per query — exactly the small-subset pattern. Redshift dc2.large always-on is expensive for ad-hoc, infrequent queries. EMR-Presto is heavier ops. Aurora is OLTP, not for 30 TB analytics scans.

question: A media-streaming platform serves video manifests and segments from S3 via CloudFront. Latency for the manifest GET is critical because it sits on the playback hot path. Engineers see 80ms of latency per manifest GET in some Regions despite CloudFront. What change most directly reduces this?
options: A. Add a CloudFront cache policy that caches the manifest with a short TTL (e.g., 2 seconds), so subsequent viewers in the same edge get cache hits.
B. Change S3 storage class to S3 Express One Zone.
C. Increase CloudFront price-class to all-edge.
D. Front S3 with API Gateway.
explanation: Manifests are tiny and shared across many simultaneous viewers; even a 1-2s edge cache TTL turns thousands of origin GETs into one, slashing per-viewer latency on cache hits. Express One Zone reduces origin latency only on cache misses — it doesn't address the bulk of viewer latency. All-edge price-class doesn't help if requests still go to origin. API Gateway in front of S3 adds latency.

question: A social network's user-feed service runs on Lambda behind API Gateway. p99 cold-start is 1.5s, hurting user-perceived latency at low traffic times. Which mitigation is appropriate?
options: A. Configure provisioned concurrency on the Lambda function for the expected baseline load.
B. Switch the runtime from Python to Go and call it done.
C. Increase function memory to 10 GB.
D. Use API Gateway caching at 60-second TTL on the response.
explanation: Provisioned concurrency keeps initialized Lambda environments warm, so requests hit zero cold-starts; that's the AWS-recommended fix. Switching runtimes can help (Go has fast cold start) but is a bigger code change and isn't always sufficient. 10 GB memory makes invocation faster but doesn't eliminate cold starts. API Gateway response caching helps repeat reads but personalized feeds typically aren't cacheable.

question: A retail company runs an Aurora MySQL writer + 3 readers. Write performance is fine but heavy reporting queries from the analytics team are starving end-user reads. Which change separates these workloads cheaply?
options: A. Create a custom Aurora reader endpoint pointing to specific reader instances reserved for reporting queries.
B. Vertically scale the writer.
C. Move reporting queries to the writer endpoint.
D. Convert the cluster to RDS for MySQL Multi-AZ.
explanation: Custom endpoints in Aurora let you carve up the reader fleet so reporting traffic hits dedicated readers and end-user reads hit others, isolating noisy-neighbour effects without scaling the writer. Vertically scaling the writer doesn't help reads. Sending more load to the writer makes things worse. RDS for MySQL has no equivalent custom-endpoint feature.

question: A scientific-imaging firm processes ~500 GB raw images per day. Pipeline reads sequentially, then random-access writes the labelled output. The pipeline currently uses gp3 EBS volumes; throughput maxes out near 1,000 MB/s and the team needs 4 GB/s sustained throughput per instance. Which storage choice fits?
options: A. io2 Block Express volumes attached to a Nitro-based instance.
B. st1 (throughput-optimized HDD) volumes.
C. EFS in performance mode 'general purpose'.
D. S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval mounted via a file gateway.
explanation: io2 Block Express on Nitro supports up to 4,000 MB/s and sub-millisecond p99 latency on a single volume — exactly the high-throughput, low-latency mixed-IO pattern. st1 is throughput-friendly but only ~500 MB/s and HDDs are bad for random-write. EFS general-purpose maxes at lower throughput and has higher latency. Glacier is archival storage with retrieval latency in seconds-to-hours.

question: A real-time-bidding system needs sub-5ms p99 read latency on ~50 GB of bidder profile data, with high read concurrency. DynamoDB has 9ms p99. Which adjustment most directly meets the latency requirement?
options: A. Front DynamoDB with DAX, hitting an in-memory cache for sub-millisecond reads.
B. Move the data to ElastiCache for Redis cluster-mode enabled in front of DynamoDB.
C. Increase the DynamoDB table's RCUs.
D. Switch the DynamoDB table to a global table.
explanation: DAX is purpose-built for DynamoDB sub-millisecond reads with the same access patterns and SDK; both DAX and Redis-in-front-of-DDB hit the latency goal, but DAX requires zero application logic for cache-coherency. Boosting RCUs doesn't lower per-call latency materially. Global tables solve cross-Region availability, not single-Region latency. Between A and B the question targets the AWS-canonical answer (DAX) for DynamoDB acceleration with the smallest code change.

question: A geospatial analytics SaaS serves precomputed map tiles via CloudFront from an S3 origin. Tiles are accessed unpredictably worldwide, with some becoming hot for a few hours then cold. The team wants to cap CloudFront cache miss latency to the origin even when an obscure tile becomes hot. Which feature applies?
options: A. CloudFront Origin Shield in front of the S3 origin.
B. CloudFront geo-restriction.
C. S3 Transfer Acceleration on the origin bucket.
D. CloudFront field-level encryption.
explanation: Origin Shield places a single regional cache layer in front of the origin so when many edges miss for the same hot tile, only ONE request hits S3 — collapsing cache-miss tail latency and origin load. Geo-restriction blocks regions, irrelevant here. S3 Transfer Acceleration is for client uploads/downloads to S3 directly. Field-level encryption is unrelated.

question: A food-delivery service writes 25,000 events/sec to a Kinesis Data Stream with 5 shards. Producers are getting ProvisionedThroughputExceededException intermittently. Which step most directly resolves this?
options: A. Add more shards to match the per-shard 1 MB/s, 1,000 records/sec ingest limit (or use On-Demand mode).
B. Increase the producer's maxRetries.
C. Switch to SQS standard queue.
D. Decrease record size below 1 KB.
explanation: Kinesis shards cap at 1 MB/s or 1,000 records/sec ingest each; 25,000 events/sec needs at least 25 shards (or use the on-demand mode that auto-scales). More retries doesn't raise the ceiling. SQS is a different model with different ordering/throughput tradeoffs. Reducing record size from already-small payloads doesn't change the records/sec limit.

question: A data-engineering team queries a 5 TB Redshift cluster. Most queries scan only the last 30 days of data but full-table scans currently run. Which Redshift change most reduces query time?
options: A. Use AUTO sort keys on the timestamp column and analyze table statistics regularly so range-restricted scans skip blocks.
B. Add a USER role with read-only permissions.
C. Increase node count from 4 to 8.
D. Migrate to Aurora MySQL.
explanation: Sort keys (especially AUTO) let Redshift's zone maps prune blocks outside the scanned timestamp range, transforming full scans into range-restricted scans. Permissions don't affect query time. More nodes give linear speedup but cost more and don't fix the underlying scan-everything-pattern. Aurora MySQL is OLTP and slower for big analytical scans.

question: A telemetry SaaS receives bursts of 200,000 small messages/sec; current SNS->Lambda pattern is hitting throttles. Order matters per device but not across devices. Which design fits?
options: A. Replace SNS with Kinesis Data Streams partitioned on device-id; consumers process per shard in order.
B. Use SQS FIFO with a single message group.
C. Move to MSK with one partition.
D. Use EventBridge with a fixed concurrency target.
explanation: Kinesis with device-id as partition key gives per-device ordering with horizontal scalability via shard count, which matches the workload shape exactly. SQS FIFO with one message-group caps at low throughput and serializes everything. MSK with one partition serializes everything too. EventBridge concurrency targets aren't designed for 200k msg/s ordered.

question: A SaaS file-collab product uses an Aurora cluster behind RDS Proxy. As the user base grew, applications report 'too many connections' during pod-restart storms. Which RDS Proxy capability addresses this?
options: A. Connection pooling and reuse — RDS Proxy maintains a pool of warm connections to the database, multiplexing them across many client connections.
B. Automatic sharding.
C. Cross-Region read replicas.
D. Query result caching.
explanation: RDS Proxy's core value is database connection pooling — it absorbs short-lived bursts from many clients and shares far fewer underlying DB connections, eliminating 'too many connections' during pod churn. RDS Proxy doesn't shard, doesn't replicate cross-Region, and doesn't cache results.

question: A document-search backend uses OpenSearch on a 3-node m6g.large.search cluster. After indexing 100 GB of new docs, queries slow drastically. CPU is fine; disk reads are saturated. Which adjustment is appropriate?
options: A. Increase shard count from 1 to a balanced number per node and ensure indices use the recommended 10-50 GB per shard target with hot/warm tiers.
B. Move to t3.small.search instances.
C. Disable replicas.
D. Increase JVM heap to 90% of RAM.
explanation: Disk-saturated reads on a low-shard-count cluster mean queries can't parallelize across nodes. The fix is shard sizing per AWS guidance (10-50 GB per shard) and considering hot/warm tiers for older data. Smaller t3 instances make I/O worse. Disabling replicas hurts availability and read parallelism. JVM heap above 50% (or ~31 GB) of RAM is explicitly NOT recommended.

question: A consumer mobile app needs to deliver static images to users globally with the lowest possible Time-to-First-Byte. Images are stored in S3 in us-east-1. Which choice helps the most?
options: A. Put a CloudFront distribution in front of the S3 bucket with appropriate cache TTLs.
B. Enable Cross-Region Replication of the bucket to ap-southeast-2.
C. Increase the bucket's request rate via prefix design.
D. Move the bucket to a Wavelength Zone.
explanation: CloudFront caches at 600+ edge locations worldwide so the request never has to traverse to us-east-1 on a cache hit — that's the lowest TTFB you can get for static content. CRR alone doesn't direct users to the nearest copy and adds storage cost. Prefix-rate is for high-RPS S3 patterns, not latency. Wavelength Zones are for 5G/edge compute, not consumer mobile static delivery globally.

question: A B2B API platform has heavy compute Lambda functions (image transforms) hitting 15-minute max execution and OOM at 10 GB. Which architecture fits without giving up the serverless cost model entirely?
options: A. Move the transform workload to AWS Fargate with task auto-scaling driven by the SQS backlog.
B. Increase Lambda memory to 32 GB.
C. Use Lambda with provisioned concurrency.
D. Replace Lambda with API Gateway HTTP API.
explanation: Fargate keeps a serverless billing model (pay per task-second) without Lambda's 15-minute and 10-GB limits, and SQS-backlog-driven scaling matches bursty workloads. Lambda max memory is 10 GB, so 32 GB isn't possible. Provisioned concurrency doesn't extend memory or runtime. API Gateway is a front door, not a compute change.

question: A real-estate marketplace does 200 ms autocomplete on city names. Latency must be under 25 ms per query at p99 across 1 M city records. Which datastore is appropriate?
options: A. ElastiCache for Redis with a sorted set for prefix lookups.
B. RDS MySQL with a B-tree index.
C. DynamoDB with begins_with on the partition key.
D. S3 Select on a CSV file.
explanation: Redis sorted sets do prefix lookups in O(log n) with single-digit-millisecond latency, easily under 25ms p99 even at 1M entries. RDS B-tree indexes are slower than in-memory and add network/connection overhead. DynamoDB doesn't allow begins_with on the partition key (only sort key) and the latency target is tighter than DynamoDB without DAX. S3 Select scans the file each query — way too slow.

question: A Lambda function aggregates results from 12 internal microservice HTTP calls; total wall time is 3.2s, dominated by sequential HTTP calls. Which change most reduces wall time?
options: A. Make the 12 calls concurrently using async I/O / Promise.all and ensure the Lambda runtime has enough memory for the parallel CPU work.
B. Increase Lambda timeout to 30s.
C. Move the function to API Gateway HTTP API.
D. Add provisioned concurrency.
explanation: Sequential I/O is a code-level inefficiency; running the 12 calls concurrently turns wall-time into roughly the slowest single call instead of the sum. Increasing timeout doesn't make it faster, just allows MORE waiting. API Gateway type doesn't matter. Provisioned concurrency reduces cold-start, not steady-state latency dominated by sequential I/O.

question: A SaaS analytics product backed by Aurora needs a copy of production data refreshed every hour into a sandbox cluster for ML training, with minimal performance impact on production. Which approach fits?
options: A. Aurora cloning, which uses copy-on-write so the sandbox is provisioned in seconds without copying all data, run hourly.
B. mysqldump production hourly and import into the sandbox.
C. Cross-Region read replicas as the sandbox.
D. AWS DMS continuous replication.
explanation: Aurora cloning is copy-on-write, near-instant, and doesn't impact the source — perfect for hourly ML-training snapshots. mysqldump locks/loads the source; for big DBs it's slow and impactful. Cross-Region replicas aren't really a 'fresh hourly cut.' DMS continuous replication is also viable but is a much bigger setup with ongoing costs versus a one-API-call clone.

question: A large publisher's static blog gets one viral spike per day to 50,000 RPS for 10 minutes; otherwise it's at 200 RPS. The blog is built as a static site in S3. Which architecture handles spikes most cheaply and quickly?
options: A. CloudFront in front of the S3 bucket with long TTLs on the HTML pages and assets.
B. EC2 web servers in an Auto Scaling group fronted by ALB.
C. ECS Fargate service with target-tracking on RPS.
D. Lambda@Edge generating each page on demand.
explanation: Static content + CloudFront with sane TTLs handles 50,000 RPS at the edge with virtually no origin load and minimal cost — the textbook spike pattern. ASG/ECS-Fargate add cost AND scale latency. Lambda@Edge per page is needlessly expensive when the content is static.

question: A SaaS company's bill is dominated by EC2 m5.xlarge usage that's stable 24/7 for at least 3 years. Which purchase option saves the most without architectural changes?
options: A. 3-year all-upfront Compute Savings Plans.
B. 1-year no-upfront Convertible RIs.
C. Spot Instances.
D. On-Demand with Trusted Advisor right-sizing.
explanation: 3-year all-upfront Compute Savings Plans give the deepest discount (up to ~66% off On-Demand) and are flexible across instance family/Region — exactly the fit for predictable 24/7 use. 1-year no-upfront Convertible RIs save less. Spot is unsuitable for stable production workloads requiring 100% uptime. Right-sizing helps but doesn't substitute for committed pricing.

question: A media archive holds 800 TB of raw footage in S3 Standard. Files are read perhaps once or twice a year. Retrieval can take up to 12 hours when needed. Which storage class is most cost-effective?
options: A. S3 Glacier Deep Archive.
B. S3 Standard-IA.
C. S3 Intelligent-Tiering.
D. S3 One Zone-IA.
explanation: Glacier Deep Archive has the lowest storage cost (around $0.00099/GB-month) and a 12-hour standard retrieval matches the access SLA. Standard-IA is cheaper than Standard but still ~6x Deep Archive. Intelligent-Tiering caps savings unless objects are rarely accessed AND it costs monitoring fees. One Zone-IA is for non-critical data and still costs more than Deep Archive.

question: An ML training fleet runs nightly for 6 hours on g5.4xlarge GPUs. Jobs are restartable and can survive 2-minute interruptions. The team currently uses On-Demand and is over budget. Which change most cuts cost?
options: A. Move the fleet to Spot with a checkpoint-and-resume pattern.
B. Switch to On-Demand Reserved Instances for 1 year.
C. Resize to g5.xlarge.
D. Run training during the day instead.
explanation: Spot offers 60-90% discount; restartable, checkpointable training jobs are the canonical fit. RIs save less than Spot and require predictable shape commitments — wasteful at 6h/day. Resizing only matters if the smaller instance can complete training in the window (probably not on a memory-bound model). Time-of-day doesn't change EC2 pricing.

question: A growing startup's NAT Gateway data-processing cost is the second biggest line item. Egress traffic is mostly Lambda functions in private subnets calling S3 and DynamoDB. Which change reduces NAT cost most directly?
options: A. Add VPC Gateway Endpoints for S3 and DynamoDB so traffic to those services bypasses the NAT gateway entirely.
B. Switch from NAT Gateway to NAT Instances on t3.small.
C. Use a single NAT Gateway across all AZs.
D. Enable VPC peering across accounts.
explanation: Gateway endpoints for S3/DynamoDB are FREE and route those services' traffic without traversing NAT, eliminating the data-processing charge entirely for that traffic. NAT Instances reintroduce ops burden and aren't necessarily cheaper at modest scale. Single-AZ NAT cuts redundancy, not data-processing cost. VPC peering doesn't apply to AWS-service traffic.

question: An e-commerce site has 30 production Aurora clusters running at single-digit CPU utilization most days but spiking on Black Friday. Which approach is most cost-aware?
options: A. Move appropriate clusters to Aurora Serverless v2, which scales ACUs continuously and bills only for capacity used.
B. Reserve max-size Aurora capacity for the year.
C. Stop the clusters every night.
D. Move to RDS for MySQL Multi-AZ.
explanation: Aurora Serverless v2 scales capacity in fine-grained ACUs and bills only what's used, ideal for highly-variable utilization with rare spikes. Year-round reservation of peak capacity is the opposite of cost-aware. Stopping at night may hurt availability and only saves a fraction. RDS MySQL doesn't have the same elastic scaling.

question: A SaaS team's CloudWatch Logs bill has tripled in 6 months. Investigation shows two log groups account for 70% of the cost from verbose application debug logs. Which combination cuts cost effectively?
options: A. Lower the retention on the verbose log groups, set application log levels to INFO in production, and configure subscription filters to send sampled debug logs to S3 only when needed.
B. Enable CloudWatch Logs Insights on those groups.
C. Move all logs to OpenSearch Service.
D. Buy a CloudWatch Savings Plan.
explanation: Cost in CloudWatch Logs is dominated by ingestion volume; log-level INFO + shorter retention + selective sampling to cheap S3 directly reduces ingest. Logs Insights ADDS query cost. OpenSearch is more expensive than CloudWatch Logs at this scale. There is no CloudWatch Logs Savings Plan.

question: A financial data warehouse on Redshift runs 9-5 weekdays and is idle outside those hours, but the team forgot it scaled up for end-of-quarter. Bill is 3x expected. Which feature most directly fixes this going forward?
options: A. Migrate to Redshift Serverless, which auto-scales compute and charges per second when active.
B. Buy 3-year Redshift Reserved Nodes at peak size.
C. Use Redshift Spectrum exclusively.
D. Move to EMR.
explanation: Redshift Serverless auto-scales compute capacity and bills per second of use; idle time costs nothing. Reserving peak nodes for 3 years makes the over-provisioning permanent. Spectrum queries S3 from Redshift, not a substitute for the cluster. EMR is a different engine with different operational and cost characteristics, not a drop-in replacement.

question: A sports-streaming startup's egress cost from S3 to viewers is dominating the bill. Most viewers consume the same set of popular live events. Which change reduces egress cost?
options: A. Front S3 with CloudFront so cached responses are served at lower per-GB egress rates and many requests don't hit S3 at all.
B. Switch to S3 One Zone-IA.
C. Enable S3 Transfer Acceleration.
D. Move the bucket to a closer Region.
explanation: CloudFront's per-GB egress price is lower than S3's, AND cache hits don't incur S3 GET fees or S3-to-CloudFront-Origin transfer cost — double savings on hot content. One Zone-IA reduces storage cost, not egress. Transfer Acceleration RAISES egress cost. Region change moves the source closer but doesn't beat edge caching.

question: An EdTech platform uses Lambda for a batch nightly job that runs 90 minutes total over 4,000 invocations; functions are CPU-bound and currently configured at 512 MB. Tests show 2,048 MB cuts wall-time per invocation by 70%. Which is most cost-aware?
options: A. Increase memory to 2,048 MB; total Lambda cost may drop because billed GB-seconds decrease when wall-time falls more than memory rises.
B. Stay at 512 MB and add provisioned concurrency.
C. Move to Step Functions Standard.
D. Run the job on a t3.micro EC2 instance.
explanation: Lambda CPU scales with memory; if 4x memory yields 70% less wall-time, GB-seconds drops to ~1.2x memory * 0.3x time = ~0.36x cost — a clear win. Provisioned concurrency adds idle-billing cost without speeding up the job. Step Functions adds per-state-transition cost. t3.micro is too underpowered for CPU-bound batch and removes Lambda's pay-per-use shape.

question: A B2B reporting product has hundreds of forgotten unattached EBS volumes, old snapshots, and unattached Elastic IPs across 30 accounts. Which AWS service most directly surfaces this waste with recommendations?
options: A. AWS Trusted Advisor cost-optimization checks (idle and underutilized resources).
B. AWS WAF.
C. AWS Backup.
D. Amazon Detective.
explanation: Trusted Advisor's cost-optimization checks specifically flag idle load balancers, underutilized EC2, unattached EBS volumes, low-utilization RDS, and idle Elastic IPs. WAF is security. AWS Backup manages backups, doesn't surface waste. Detective is for security investigation.

question: A startup's monthly bill spiked from $4k to $19k after onboarding a partner integration. The engineering manager wants to know exactly which service and account drove the increase, broken down by tag. Which tool is the right starting point?
options: A. AWS Cost Explorer with cost-allocation tags activated, filtered by linked account and grouped by service.
B. CloudWatch metrics on the EstimatedCharges namespace.
C. AWS Budgets actions.
D. Trusted Advisor.
explanation: Cost Explorer is the analysis tool; with cost-allocation tags activated you can pivot by account, service, and tag — which is the question being asked. EstimatedCharges metrics give a single-account total without breakdown. Budgets actions are for thresholds/alerts, not investigation. Trusted Advisor surfaces waste, not detailed cost attribution.

question: A digital-publisher's CMS runs on EC2 with attached EBS gp2 volumes provisioned at 5 TB each but using less than 100 IOPS. Which migration most reduces cost without performance loss?
options: A. Migrate the volumes to gp3 and provision IOPS/throughput separately at the actual usage levels.
B. Migrate to io2 Block Express.
C. Move data to EFS.
D. Convert to magnetic standard volumes.
explanation: gp3 is roughly 20% cheaper than gp2 at the same size and decouples IOPS/throughput from size, so you don't pay for unneeded IOPS that gp2 bundles by capacity. io2 Block Express is more expensive. EFS is a different access pattern and pricing model. Magnetic standard is end-of-life for new volumes.

question: A SaaS firm's multi-AZ RDS for PostgreSQL clusters are on a Reserved Instance from 2022 that doesn't fit current usage; 40% of capacity is wasted. Which option recovers value with the least disruption?
options: A. List the unused portion of the RIs on the AWS Reserved Instance Marketplace.
B. Cancel the RIs.
C. Switch to Spot Instances for RDS.
D. Wait until expiration.
explanation: Standard EC2 RIs (and some RDS RIs by program) can be sold on the Marketplace to recoup partial value. Cancellation isn't typically allowed. RDS doesn't support Spot. Waiting recovers nothing.

question: A multi-account organization is shifting from per-account RIs to Compute Savings Plans for flexibility. Leadership asks which Org-level setting maximizes Savings-Plans utilization across accounts. Which configuration is appropriate?
options: A. Enable Savings Plans discount sharing in AWS Organizations so unused commitment in one account benefits other accounts.
B. Disable consolidated billing.
C. Buy Savings Plans separately in each member account.
D. Move all workloads to the management account.
explanation: Savings Plans discount sharing (default ON, but worth verifying) lets unused commitment in any account be applied to eligible usage in any other account in the Organization, maximizing utilization. Disabling consolidated billing breaks pooling entirely. Per-account purchases waste idle commitment. Concentrating workloads in the management account is operationally bad.

question: A retailer's S3 bucket holds 4 PB of mixed-access data: 30% accessed hourly, 50% accessed monthly, 20% rarely. Access patterns shift unpredictably. The team doesn't want to write lifecycle rules per prefix. Which storage class fits?
options: A. S3 Intelligent-Tiering with the Archive Access and Deep Archive Access tiers enabled.
B. S3 Standard for all 4 PB.
C. S3 Standard-IA for all 4 PB.
D. S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval for all 4 PB.
explanation: Intelligent-Tiering automatically moves objects between Frequent, Infrequent, Archive, and Deep Archive tiers based on observed access — perfect for unpredictable mixed patterns and removes lifecycle-rule maintenance. Standard wastes money on idle data. Standard-IA charges retrieval fees on the hot 30% which would be expensive. Glacier Flexible has minute-to-hour retrieval — bad for the hot subset.

question: A logistics company runs a Kinesis Data Stream at 50 shards 24/7, but utilization averages 15% and rarely exceeds 40%. Which change reduces cost while preserving headroom for the spikes?
options: A. Switch the stream to On-Demand mode, which auto-scales capacity and bills per data ingested without managing shards.
B. Reduce the stream to 5 shards permanently.
C. Move to MSK with the smallest broker.
D. Replace with SQS standard queue.
explanation: Kinesis On-Demand mode auto-scales and only charges for actual throughput plus a base fee, ideal for low-average / occasional-peak workloads. 5 shards permanently removes spike headroom. MSK is a different engine and brokers carry their own minimums. SQS doesn't preserve ordered partitioning by key like Kinesis.
